elements.sent-2411-008: Traditional single object tracking (SOT) aims to locate and track an arbitrary object throughout a video sequence.
elements.sent-2411-009: Recent advancements in deep learning have significantly improved tracking accuracy and success rate.
elements.sent-2411-010: However, SOT trackers heavily rely on precise initial bounding box annotations in the first frame to establish the template feature of the tracking object.

elements.sent-2411-012: Thus, the accuracy of annotation plays a crucial role in these trackers.
elements.sent-2411-013: However, annotating the tracked object within the video stream in real-time interactive scenarios introduces additional challenges.

elements.sent-2411-017: The effectiveness of single object trackers largely depends on the choice of a proper initialization method, making it crucial for practical applications.
elements.sent-2411-018: We reevaluated different initialization methods for the single object tracking task in real-time interactive scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-019: As shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:different initilization method.}
elements.sent-2411-020: This method has several issues: (1) the detector outputs multiple bounding boxes, and further interaction is required to accurately locate the target to be tracked; (2) if the detector fails to detect a target, it cannot specify a target in the current frame.
elements.sent-2411-021: Thus, detector-based initialization has significant limitations in real-time interactive scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-022: The second method, introduced in natural language tracking tasks~\cite{DBLP:conf/wacv/FengABLS20, DBLP:conf/cvpr/WangSZJW0W21, DBLP:conf/cvpr/LiYCP22, DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2303-12027, DBLP:conf/cvpr/FengABS21 , involves specifying the tracking target via a natural language description.}
elements.sent-2411-023: The main problems with this method are: (1) precisely specifying a tracking target requires an accurate natural language description, which increases the thinking time cost of interaction and decreases stability; (2) precisely locating a specific target using only a natural language description is challenging in scenarios containing many similar targets.
elements.sent-2411-024: Considering these limitations, designing a more streamlined and stable initialization method for real-time interactive scenarios is highly meaningful.
elements.sent-2411-025: Currently, the clicking interaction method~\cite{Click,chen2022point,kirillov2023segment,DBLP:Attnshift,yu2022object has garnered widespread attention from researchers.}
elements.sent-2411-026: We believe that clicking, because of its speed, simplicity and precision, is particularly well-suited as an initialization method for single-object trackers in real-time interactive scenarios.

elements.sent-2411-079: In this section, we first review the general setting of single object tracking and introduce the ClickTrack paradigm in detail.
elements.sent-2411-080: Then, we design two simple baseline models with reference to two well-known object detectors, Faster R-CNN~\cite{ren2015faster and FCOS~\cite{tian2019fcos .}}

elements.sent-2411-083: Traditional single object tracking (SOT)~\cite{yan2021learning, bertinetto2016fully,li2018high,li2019siamrpn++ typically requires an accurate bounding box annotation for the target object in the initial frame of a video sequence.}
elements.sent-2411-084: Given the initial object bounding box, denoted as $b_0$, and the tracking sequence, denoted as $S$, the tracker $\mathcal{T}$ utilizes the input to produce tracking results for subsequent frames.
elements.sent-2411-085: The tracking process can be described as follows: \begin{equation}\label{equ:traditional} \mathbf{b}=[b_1,\dots,b_n]=\mathcal{T}(S, b_0),\end{equation}where $b_i$ denotes the bounding box of the tracked object predicted in the $i$-th frame.
elements.sent-2411-086: In practical application scenarios, the initialization of $b_0$ is essential for single-object trackers.
elements.sent-2411-087: However, as described in Section \ref{intro , the various initialization methods currently available exhibit certain limitations in real-time interactive scenarios.}
elements.sent-2411-088: We believe clicking is more suitable as the interactive method for real-time initialization of single-object trackers.
elements.sent-2411-089: Consequently, the tracking process is transformed as follows: Specifically, we denote the point annotation in the initial frame as $p_0$ and introduce a point-to-box regressor, denoted as $\mathcal{R}$.
elements.sent-2411-090: Consequently, the single object tracking paradigm can be transformed as follows: \begin{equation}\label{equ:traditional} \mathbf{b}=\mathcal{T}(S, \mathcal{R}({p_0})).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-091: In this way, no specific modifications to existing trackers are required, and our module can simply connect to them.
elements.sent-2411-092: We name this new SOT paradigm ClickTrack.
elements.sent-2411-093: The main challenge of ClickTrack is to discover the whole object from an initial point and use a bounding box to represent the object.
elements.sent-2411-094: Then the estimated bounding box can be viewed as the template for the tracker and track the object in the following frames.
elements.sent-2411-095: It is obvious that the regression outputs for object bounding boxes in conventional object detectors align well with our proposed design.
elements.sent-2411-096: Therefore, inspired by classic object detectors Faster R-CNN~\cite{ren2015faster and FCOS~\cite{tian2019fcos , we first design two naive point-based regressors to tackle the challenge.}}
elements.sent-2411-097: We first define the objective of the regression: the distance from the labeled point to the four edges of the object bounding box.
elements.sent-2411-098: Specifically, the distance to each edge of the bounding box is predicted from the labeled point $p_0=(p_x, p_y)$.
elements.sent-2411-099: The prediction is denoted as $delta=(\hat{l ,\hat{t ,\hat{r ,\hat{b )$, representing the distances in the left, top, right, and bottom directions, respectively.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-100: Then, the point can be converted into a bounding box $b=(x_{0 ,y_{0 ,x_{1 ,y_{1 )$: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned}\label{equation: convert box} x_{0}=p_x - \hat{l}, \quad y_{0}=p_y - \hat{t}, x_{1}=p_x + \hat{r}, \quad y_{1}=p_y + \hat{b}. \end{aligned}\end{equation} In this paper, we study how to obtain a high-quality bounding box with point annotation.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-101: We define $I$ as the input image, $\mathcal{F (I)$ as the backbone features from the image encoder.}

elements.sent-2411-107: An alternative strategy is to utilize the point feature at the location of the labeled point from the whole image features $\mathcal{F (I)$, denoted as $f_{point}$.}

elements.sent-2411-130: We designed the Prototype Selection (PS) module to select the most suitable initial regression range for each initial point.

elements.sent-2411-229: In this section, we first explain the design motivation behind GCR-SAM, followed by a description of its overall structure.
elements.sent-2411-230: Finally, we present the main experimental results of GCR-SAM.
elements.sent-2411-231: Recently, the powerful segmentation ability of the visual large model Segment Anything Model (SAM) has gained widespread attention.
elements.sent-2411-232: SAM can accept three types of inputs, including the entire image, boxes and points.
elements.sent-2411-233: The input form of accepting points allows SAM to meet the task of VOS initialization in real-time interactive scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-234: However, we believe that SAM also faces ambiguity issues when receiving single-point inputs.
elements.sent-2411-235: In order to further explore the ability of the proposed GCR structure to eliminate the problem of single-point ambiguity, we combine the GCR head as a plugin with SAM, naming GCR-SAM.
elements.sent-2411-236: This combination empowers GCR with the strong segmentation ability of SAM, extending GCR's ability to produce mask-level results.
elements.sent-2411-237: In this section, we present the comprehensive framework of GCR-SAM.
elements.sent-2411-238: As depicted in Fig.~\ref{fig:tcr-SAM framework , the framework of GCR-SAM is straightforward.}
elements.sent-2411-239: We retain the complete architecture of SAM, including the image encoder, prompt encoder, and mask decoder.
elements.sent-2411-240: During the training process, we keep SAM's pre-trained parameters frozen.
elements.sent-2411-241: Leveraging the non-ambiguity nature of SAM when accepting bounding boxes as prompt input, we integrated GCR's Prototype Selection (PS) and Iterative Regression (IR) modules as plugins into SAM.
elements.sent-2411-242: The two modules are represented as GCR head uniformly in Fig.~\ref{fig:tcr-SAM framework .}
elements.sent-2411-243: The GCR head can produce bounding boxes related to the input text, which serve as prompts for SAM, thereby generating mask results relevant to the input text.
elements.sent-2411-244: By integrating GCR with SAM, we successfully alleviate the ambiguity issues arising from single-point input.

elements.sent-2411-284: In this paper, we rethink the SOT paradigm in real-time interactive scenarios and propose a new paradigm named ClickTrack, which aims to track arbitrary objects by clicking.
elements.sent-2411-285: We design the GCR model, which can convert the click point to a bounding box.
elements.sent-2411-286: GCR can be combined with arbitrary trackers to meet the needs of the real-time interactive scenario.
elements.sent-2411-287: Furthermore, we introduce text to GCR with a novel structure Guided Convolution (GC), which can greatly eliminate the ambiguity issue caused by the click interaction mode.
elements.sent-2411-288: Additionally, we combine GCR with SAM, and by introducing text, the ambiguity problem when SAM receives single-point input is greatly alleviated.
